title: Translation symmetry-enforced long-range entanglement in mixed states
authors: Ryan Thorngren, Lei Gioia, Carolyn Zhang
abstract: We show by a counting argument that even though translation symmetry admits symmetric short-range entangled (SRE) eigenstates, there are not enough such SRE eigenstates to span the zero momentum sector. This means that the fixed point strong-to-weak spontaneous symmetry breaking state of translation symmetry is long-range entangled: it cannot be written as a mixture of SRE states. This is a subtle form of long-range entanglement in mixed states that cannot be detected by long-range connected correlation functions.
summary: The paper demonstrates that translation symmetry can force a mixed state to possess long-range entanglement that cannot be decomposed into short-range entangled components. This effect is particularly interesting because it remains hidden from standard local correlation function measurements. The study provides a rigorous lower bound on the circuit depth required to simulate such states.
main_problem: The paper investigates whether translation symmetry can enforce long-range entanglement (LRE) in mixed states, specifically determining if the maximally mixed state of translation-invariant states is LRE.
main_result: The authors prove that the maximally mixed state of translation-invariant states is LRE because the dimension of the zero momentum sector exceeds the dimension of the space spanned by short-range entangled (SRE) states. This LRE is subtle because it cannot be detected by long-range connected correlation functions.
method: A dimension-based counting argument is used to compare the size of the zero momentum sector to the space of SRE states, supported by a 'Tails Lemma' and circuit decomposition into Matrix Product Operators.
model_or_system: 1D quantum chains of length n with periodic boundary conditions and local Hilbert space dimension q, focusing on the zero momentum sector of translation-invariant states.
key_observables: Trace distance (to measure approximation fidelity), connected correlation functions, and circuit depth/evolution time.
important_parameters: System size (n), local dimension (q), circuit depth (d), and evolution time (tau).
main_assumptions: The primary results are derived for 1D systems; the proof assumes translation symmetry of the resulting state but not necessarily the generating circuit.
figures_summary: A circuit diagram illustrating a depth-4 circuit with a highlighted light-cone (sub-circuit C0) and internal bonds representing Schmidt decompositions.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the problem of LRE in mixed states, introduces a counting argument and dimension bounds, develops a mathematical framework using circuit decomposition and MPS counting, provides the main theorem on LRE, and discusses the implications for correlation functions and higher dimensions.
why_interesting: This is highly relevant for many-body dynamics as it establishes a fundamental link between symmetry, entanglement structure, and the complexity (circuit depth) required for thermalization and state preparation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
